title: Moral Conscience
html:
The Big Question

What is that inner voice that tells me “this is right” or “this is wrong”? Can I trust it?

It was 1875. Cardinal John Henry Newman, the most famous English convert of the nineteenth century, received a letter from the Duke of Norfolk asking what he thought about conscience after the definition of papal infallibility at the First Vatican Council. The question was explosive: if the Pope is infallible, what room is left for personal conscience? Newman replied with a letter that would become a classic of moral theology. And in it he wrote a sentence that still sends a shiver down the spine:

“Certainly, if I am obliged to bring religion into after-dinner toasts —which indeed does not seem quite the thing— I shall drink —to the Pope, if you please —still, to Conscience first, and to the Pope afterwards.”

— Letter to the Duke of Norfolk, 1875

Was Newman placing conscience above the Pope? Not exactly. He was saying something far deeper: conscience is not a private opinion or a whimsical feeling. It is the voice of God in the soul. It is the “aboriginal Vicar of Christ,” the first representative of God in each person. The Pope is the Vicar of Christ for the universal Church; conscience is the Vicar of Christ in the inner sanctuary of every human being. And if conscience is well-formed, it will never contradict the Pope, because both drink from the same source: God’s truth.

But Newman also knew —and he taught it with absolute clarity— that conscience is not infallible. It can be mistaken. It can be malformed, deformed, numbed, or corrupted. That is why the formation of conscience is one of the most urgent tasks of the Christian life. It does no good to have an infallible Pope if my conscience is lying to me.

This lesson is about that: understanding what conscience is, how it is formed, when I must obey it, and when I must distrust it. Because conscience is not a luxury for saints or an abstract topic for theologians. It is the rudder of our moral life. And every day, dozens of times, it is telling us something. The question is whether we are listening.

Seed — Personal Connection

Imagine that inside you there is a light that turns on when something is good and turns off when something is bad. That light is your conscience. God placed it there to guide you, like a friend who whispers in your ear: “go this way” or “don’t go that way.” Sometimes we listen to that voice and we feel at peace. Other times we ignore it and feel a weight in our heart.

Think and share: Can you remember a time when you felt that “something inside you” was telling you not to do something? What happened? How did you feel afterward?

1. What Is Conscience? The Inner Sanctuary

The Catechism of the Catholic Church opens its treatment of conscience with one of the most beautiful definitions in the entire document. It does not begin with a technical formula, but with an image: conscience is a sanctuary:

“Deep within his conscience man discovers a law which he has not laid upon himself but which he must obey. Its voice, ever calling him to love and to do what is good and to avoid evil, sounds in his heart at the right moment. For man has in his heart a law inscribed by God. His conscience is man’s most secret core and his sanctuary. There he is alone with God whose voice echoes in his depths.”

— CCC 1776, citing Gaudium et Spes 16

Let us pause a moment on every word of this definition. The Catechism says that conscience is man’s “most secret core” and his “sanctuary.” “Sanctuary” is the word we use for the place where the Eucharist is kept in a church. It is the holiest place in the temple. To say that conscience is a sanctuary means that within every person there is a sacred space, a place where God dwells and speaks. This is not a poetic metaphor; it is a theological claim. In your interior, God is speaking to you.

But the Catechism adds something crucial: in that sanctuary, man is “alone with God.” This is of enormous importance. It means that no one can enter your conscience without your permission. No human power —not the State, not a boss, not a friend, not even the Church— can force you to act against your conscience. Freedom of conscience is the first human right, because it protects the space where God speaks to you. As the Second Vatican Council stated in Dignitatis Humanae:

“The human person has a right to religious freedom. This freedom means that all men are to be immune from coercion on the part of individuals or of social groups and of any human power… no one is to be forced to act in a manner contrary to his own conscience.”

— Dignitatis Humanae 2

But caution: that conscience is an inviolable sanctuary does not mean it is infallible. It means that God respects our freedom so much that even when our conscience is mistaken, He speaks to us through it and expects that, over time, we will form it correctly. Freedom of conscience is not freedom to invent the truth; it is freedom to seek it without coercion.

It Is Not a Feeling: It Is a Judgment of Reason

The Catechism goes further and defines conscience with technical precision:

“Moral conscience is a judgment of reason by which the human person recognizes the moral quality of a concrete act that he is going to perform, is in the process of performing, or has already completed.”

— CCC 1778

Three words are decisive here: judgment, reason, and concrete act.

Judgment: conscience is not a diffuse feeling of guilt or peace. It is a verdict. It says “this is good, do it” or “this is evil, avoid it.” It does not whisper vague things; it issues commands.

Reason: conscience belongs to the understanding, not to the feelings. It is not “what I feel” or “what my gut tells me.” It is the act of applying objective moral principles to the concrete situation before me. It is an intellectual act, not an emotional one —though emotions may accompany it.

Concrete act: conscience does not judge abstract theories; it judges specific actions. It does not tell me “lying is bad” in general; it tells me “this excuse you are about to give is a lie, do not say it.” It is practical, not theoretical.

Many people confuse conscience with other things. Let us see what it is not:

  • It is not a feeling. I can feel perfectly good doing something objectively bad, and I can feel terrible doing something objectively good. Feelings deceive; a well-formed conscience does not.
  • It is not the superego. Freud spoke of the “superego” as the interiorized voice of parents and society. Conscience is not that. It is not simply “what I was taught as a child.” It is the capacity to discover moral truth for myself, illuminated by reason and faith.
  • It is not public opinion. “Everyone does it” does not make something wrong into something right. Conscience does not take polls. Sometimes it will tell you exactly the opposite of what the majority says.
  • It is not an autonomous god. Conscience does not create moral truth; it discovers it. I cannot say “my conscience tells me this is good” and pretend that this makes it good. Conscience is at the service of truth, not the other way around.

Sprouts — Going Deeper

Think about the difference between these three voices you can hear inside you when you are about to make a decision:

  • Your parents’ voice: “I’ll get punished if I get caught.”
  • Your friends’ voice: “Everyone does it, don’t be boring.”
  • Your conscience’s voice: “This is evil. Even if no one catches you, even if everyone does it, this is evil. Do not do it.”

Which of the three do you hear most often? Which one do you think you should listen to more? Conscience is the only one of the three that comes from God. The other two may coincide with it or may be against it. But when conscience speaks, it does not ask permission from the opinion of others.

2. How Conscience Works: Before, During, and After

Conscience is not a switch that turns on once; it is a process that accompanies all human action. It operates in three moments:

Before Acting: Anticipatory Conscience

Before doing something, conscience judges: “what you are about to do is good; do it” or “what you are about to do is evil; do not do it.” It is like a guardian at the gate who examines what is about to enter. This prior judgment is the most important, because it is the one that can prevent evil before it happens. St. Paul says: “Test everything; hold fast what is good. Abstain from every form of evil” (1 Thess 5:21-22).

During the Act: Accompanying Conscience

While you act, conscience can confirm your course or give warning signals. Sometimes, in the middle of an action, we hear that voice that says: “stop” or “continue.” A person with a sensitive conscience notices these signals in real time. Someone with a numbed conscience ignores them until it is too late.

After Acting: Retrospective Conscience

After the act, conscience approves or reproves what was done. If we acted well, we feel a deep peace, a satisfaction that is not pride but rest in the truth. If we acted badly, remorse arises: an interior disquiet that accuses us. Remorse is not bad; it is conscience doing its job. What is bad is ignoring it. St. Paul describes those whose conscience is “seared” (1 Tim 4:2) —that is, so hardened by repeated sin that they no longer feel remorse. That is one of the most dangerous spiritual conditions.

St. Paul himself gives us an example of a healthy conscience when, before the Sanhedrin, he declares:

“Brethren, I have lived before God in all good conscience up to this day.”

— Acts 23:1 (cf. Acts 24:16)

Notice that Paul does not say: “I have never made a mistake.” Paul persecuted Christians before his conversion, and he himself acknowledges this as a very grave error. What he says is: “I have lived with a good conscience.” That is: when I acted, I did so according to what my conscience dictated, sincerely seeking God’s will. That is the “good conscience”: it is not about never being wrong; it is about always seeking the truth and following it.

3. The Formation of Conscience: A Lifelong Task

We arrive at the most practical point of this entire lesson. Conscience is not born formed. It is like a muscle: the more it is exercised, the stronger it becomes; the more it is neglected, the more it atrophies. The Catechism is emphatic:

“The education of the conscience is a lifelong task. The education of conscience guarantees freedom and engenders peace of heart.”

— CCC 1784

How is conscience formed? The Catechism points to several indispensable means:

1. The Word of God

Scripture is “a lamp to my feet and a light to my path” (Ps 119:105). Reading the Bible is not just acquiring information; it is exposing the conscience to the mind of God. The commandments, the beatitudes, the parables, the counsels of St. Paul… all of this sculpts the conscience according to the model of Christ. As the Letter to the Hebrews says: “the word of God is living and active, sharper than any two-edged sword, piercing to the division of soul and spirit… and discerning the thoughts and intentions of the heart” (Heb 4:12).

2. The Teaching of the Church

The Church is “the pillar and bulwark of the truth” (1 Tim 3:15). The Magisterium is not an enemy of conscience, but its teacher. The moral teaching of the Church does not oppress conscience; it frees it from ignorance and error. Just as a map does not take away your freedom to walk but enables you to reach your destination, so the Church’s doctrine does not suppress your conscience but orients it. The Catechism itself is one of the most powerful instruments for forming conscience.

3. Prayer and the Sacramental Life

In prayer, we ask God to illuminate our conscience. Psalm 139 is the perfect model of this attitude: “Search me, O God, and know my heart! Try me and know my thoughts! And see if there be any grievous way in me, and lead me in the way everlasting!” (Ps 139:23-24). The Eucharist nourishes the conscience with the Body of Christ; Confession heals and purifies it; the Holy Spirit, received in the sacraments, is the “interior Master” who teaches from within.

4. Examination of Conscience

One of the most practical and forgotten tools. The examination of conscience —especially in the form given to it by St. Ignatius of Loyola— is a daily review of our actions to detect patterns of sin and of grace. It is not an exercise of psychological self-flagellation; it is an encounter with truth under God’s loving gaze. Five minutes at the end of the day can transform the quality of our moral life.

5. Counsel from Prudent People

Conscience is not self-sufficient. Sometimes we need others to help us see what we cannot see. A spiritual director, a confessor, a friend with Christian maturity can be instruments of God to illuminate our conscience. Proverbs says it clearly: “In an abundance of counselors there is safety” (Prov 11:14).

6. Study and Intellectual Formation

Religious ignorance is one of the principal causes of a deformed conscience. Many Catholics have poorly formed consciences simply because they were never taught the faith seriously. Studying the Catechism, reading moral theology, forming oneself in the Church’s social doctrine… all of this is not an intellectual luxury; it is an obligation of every Christian who wants a healthy conscience.

Root — Historical Context

The formation of conscience is especially urgent in our age, when we are exposed to a massive quantity of contradictory messages. Social media, television series, advertising, influencers… all of this forms —or deforms— our conscience constantly, often without our realizing it. What is forming your conscience right now? What are you consuming each day: content that illuminates your moral sense or content that darkens it?

St. Thomas Aquinas taught that conscience can err in two ways: by vincible ignorance (when we could have known the truth but did not make the effort) and by invincible ignorance (when we genuinely had no way to know the truth). The former does not excuse us from sin; the latter does, but it does not turn evil into good. This balance is fundamental to understanding moral responsibility.

4. Types of Conscience: When the Compass Misfires

Not all consciences function correctly. The Catechism distinguishes several types, and knowing them is essential for spiritual self-diagnosis.

Certain Conscience

This is the conscience that judges without doubt, with moral certainty. It knows that this is good and that is evil. It is the type of conscience to which we aspire: a conscience that, illuminated by faith and reason, issues firm and confident judgments. But be careful: a conscience may be certain (it does not doubt) and yet be erroneous (it is mistaken). Subjective certainty does not guarantee objective truth.

Doubtful Conscience

This is the conscience that cannot decide whether something is good or evil. In the face of doubt, the Catechism establishes a principle: one must never act with a doubtful conscience. If you are not sure whether something is licit or not, you must resolve the doubt before acting: seek information, consult, pray. Acting with doubt is moral recklessness. “He who acts against conscience sins; he who acts with doubt has already sinned in his intention.”

Lax Conscience

This is the conscience that tends to justify everything, to downplay sin, to consider everything “not that serious.” People with a lax conscience say things like: “it’s not that big a deal,” “everyone does it,” “God is merciful and will forgive me.” It is the conscience of someone who has grown accustomed to sin and has lost sensitivity. It is the “seared” conscience St. Paul speaks of: moral callousness.

Scrupulous Conscience

This is the opposite extreme to laxism. The scrupulous person sees sin everywhere, tortures himself with doubts about whether he has sinned or not, and lives in a state of perpetual moral anxiety. Scruples may have psychological roots (anxiety, OCD) or spiritual ones (poor formation). The cure for scruples is not relaxing morality, but receiving spiritual direction and learning to trust the judgment of the confessor. Sometimes the greatest act of virtue for a scrupulous person is to obey his spiritual director even against what his scruples dictate.

Erroneous Conscience

This is the conscience that judges badly: it calls good evil and evil good. The error may be invincible (there was no way to know the truth: someone who grows up in a culture that normalizes theft and has never heard the Gospel) or vincible (he could have known the truth but did not bother to seek it: someone who could inform himself of the Church’s doctrine but does not).

“Ignorance of Christ and his Gospel, bad example given by others, enslavement to one’s passions, assertion of a mistaken notion of autonomy of conscience, rejection of the Church’s authority and her teaching, lack of conversion and of charity: these can be at the source of errors of judgment in moral conduct.”

— CCC 1792

What is crucial is to understand that we must follow our certain conscience even if it is erroneous (CCC 1790), but we have the grave obligation to form our conscience so that it is not erroneous. We cannot take refuge behind a malformed conscience to justify evil.

5. “Never Do Evil That Good May Come of It”

One of the firmest principles of Catholic morality, and one of the most violated in practice. The Catechism formulates it thus:

“One may never do evil so that good may result from it.”

— CCC 1789, citing Rom 3:8

This principle is radical. It means that the end does not justify the means. I cannot lie “to protect someone.” I cannot steal “to give to the poor.” I cannot commit abortion “to save the mother.” Intrinsic evil —that which is evil in itself, regardless of circumstances or intention— can never be chosen as a means, even if a good end is pursued.

St. Paul had to combat this error already in the early Church. Some were saying: “let us sin that grace may abound” (cf. Rom 6:1). Others were slandering him, saying he taught “let us do evil that good may come” (Rom 3:8). Paul’s response is crushing: such people “are justly condemned.”

This principle is especially relevant today. Moral relativism constantly whispers to us that what matters is the result, not the means. That if the intention is good, the action is justified. The Catechism says exactly the opposite: there are actions that are always illicit, in every circumstance and with any intention. These are the so-called intrinsically evil acts.

A conscience formed in God’s law knows how to detect these red lines and does not cross them. A malformed conscience, by contrast, crosses them and then seeks justifications.

6. The Primacy of Conscience and Its Limits

We arrive at the most delicate question and perhaps the most misunderstood of the entire teaching on conscience.

The Golden Rule: Obey Your Conscience

The Catechism affirms with absolute clarity:

“Man must always obey the certain judgment of his conscience. If he were deliberately to act against it, he would condemn himself.”

— CCC 1790

This is of capital importance. Acting against your certain conscience is always sin. Always. Even if objectively the action were good, if you are convinced it is evil, you sin by doing it. Conversely, if you follow your certain conscience, even if objectively you are mistaken, you do not sin (though the act remains objectively evil).

Example: suppose a person is convinced that eating meat on Friday is mortal sin. If he eats meat, he sins subjectively, even though objectively the Church’s law no longer binds in that manner. His erroneous conscience binds him, and he must obey it. But —and this is what many forget— he also has the obligation to form his conscience so that it ceases to be mistaken.

The Obligation to Form Conscience

It is not enough to say “I follow my conscience.” One must ask: have I done everything possible to ensure that my conscience is well-formed? If not, I am culpable for my error, and therefore my erroneous actions are imputable to me. Vincible ignorance does not excuse.

St. Thomas Aquinas explains it with precision: conscience binds, but it can bind wrongly. If my conscience tells me that X is good, I must do X. But if X is objectively evil, I am responsible for having arrived at a conscience that tells me that. Conscience is the subjective norm of morality; God’s law is the objective norm. Both must coincide, and when they do not, one must work to make them coincide.

Conscience and Authority in the Church

Here we return to Newman. Conscience is not a rival of the Magisterium; it is its interlocutor. The Church does not supplant conscience; it forms it. The Pope does not speak in place of your conscience; he speaks to illuminate it. But there are times when a Catholic may feel, in conscience, that he cannot accept a particular teaching. What then?

The Catholic answer, following Newman, is: first, examine whether you have done everything possible to understand the teaching and to form your conscience. Have you prayed? Have you studied? Have you consulted? If after all that your conscience still disagrees, you must follow it —but with humility, recognizing that you may be mistaken and that you need to continue seeking the truth. It is not an arrogant “I am right and the Church is wrong.” It is a humble “I do not see the truth of this teaching, Lord, enlighten me.”

The great witnesses of conscience —Thomas More, Newman, Bonhoeffer— followed conscience against political power, not against revealed truth. They followed conscience formed by faith, not conscience as an excuse for caprice.

7. Connection with Scripture

Scripture is full of passages that illuminate conscience. These are some of the most significant:

When Gentiles who have not the law do by nature what the law requires, they are a law to themselves, even though they do not have the law. They show that what the law requires is written on their hearts, their conscience also bearing witness, while their conflicting thoughts accuse or perhaps excuse them.

— Rom 2:14-15

This passage is fundamental. St. Paul says that even those who do not know the law of Moses have a law written on their hearts: the natural law, which conscience perceives. Conscience bears witness, sometimes accusing and sometimes defending. This is not a Christian invention; it is part of human nature created by God.

By this we shall know that we are of the truth, and reassure our hearts before him whenever our hearts condemn us; for God is greater than our hearts, and he knows everything.

— 1 Jn 3:19-20

St. John adds an important nuance: sometimes our own heart condemns us, even when God does not condemn us. Conscience can be more severe than God. That is why conscience needs to be illuminated by the truth that “God is greater than our hearts.” Not every remorse comes from the Holy Spirit.

I will put my law within them, and I will write it upon their hearts. I will be their God, and they shall be my people.

— Jer 31:33

The promise of the New Covenant is precisely this: that God’s law will no longer be something external, engraved on stone, but something interior, written on the heart. The Christian conscience is the fulfillment of this prophecy: God’s law interiorized by the Holy Spirit.

And the definitive criterion is given by St. Paul to Timothy:

“The aim of our charge is love that issues from a pure heart and a good conscience and sincere faith.”

— 1 Tim 1:5

Paul’s triple mention —pure heart, good conscience, sincere faith— shows that conscience is not an isolated faculty; it forms part of a spiritual organism where everything is connected: a purified heart, an illuminated conscience, and faith lived sincerely. Where one fails, the others fail.

8. The Examination of Conscience: The Practical Tool

It does no good to have a brilliant theory about conscience if we do not then apply it. The examination of conscience is the bridge between theology and life. St. Ignatius of Loyola turned it into a daily practice of five steps that anyone can do:

  1. Give thanks: begin by thanking God for the gifts of the day. This already forms the conscience: it accustoms it to see God’s action in ordinary life.
  2. Ask for light: ask the Holy Spirit to show you your sins without excuses, but also without scruples.
  3. Examine: review the day —actions, words, thoughts, omissions— and detect where you have failed. Do not examine yourself in the abstract (“I am impatient”) but concretely (“at 3pm I yelled at my brother”).
  4. Ask for forgiveness: repent sincerely. This is not a psychological exercise; it is an act of love: it pains me to have separated myself from You.
  5. Resolution: formulate a concrete resolution for tomorrow. Not “I will be a better person” but “when my brother irritates me, I will breathe deeply before responding.”

This examination does not replace the sacrament of Confession; it prepares for it. Confession is the place where conscience receives not only the diagnosis (you have sinned) but the cure (you are forgiven).

Tree — Living It Out

This week, commit to the following:

  1. Daily examen: Dedicate 5 minutes each night to the Ignatian examen. Do not skip a single day. By the end of the week, you will notice patterns you did not see before.
  2. Confession: If it has been more than a month since your last confession, go this week. Prepare a more detailed examination of conscience using a questionnaire (available online and in Catholic apps).
  3. Read the Catechism: Read continuously through CCC 1776-1802. Underline anything that surprises or challenges you.
  4. Prayer: Pray Psalm 139:23-24 every morning: “Search me, O God, and know my heart.”
  5. A difficult decision: Before making an important decision this week, ask yourself: “If I do this, will I be at peace with my conscience tomorrow?”
  6. Share: Talk with someone you trust about what you are learning about conscience. Teaching is the best way to learn.

For Reflection

1. What is the difference between your conscience, your feelings, and your parents’ voice in your head? How do you distinguish one from another?

Give a concrete example: a recent situation where you felt all three voices. Conscience does not shout; it whispers. But its whisper is unmistakable once you learn to recognize it.

2. Do you tend more toward a lax conscience (“everything is fine, it’s not that bad”) or a scrupulous one (“everything is a sin, I never do anything right”)? Where does that tendency come from?

Both extremes are dangerous. A lax conscience grows accustomed to sin; a scrupulous one distrusts God’s love. Both need healing. Both are treated with formation and spiritual direction.

3. When was the last time you deliberately ignored your conscience? What happened? What did you learn?

This is not about feeling paralyzing guilt but about recognizing patterns. The first step to healing conscience is to stop lying to it. Be brutally honest with yourself.

4. Of the six ways to form conscience (Word of God, Church teaching, prayer, examination, counsel, study), which is the most neglected in your life? What concrete step can you take this week to strengthen it?

Do not try to tackle them all at once. Choose one and follow through. Constancy in small things transforms more than grand resolutions that are abandoned.

5. Imagine a friend tells you: “My conscience says this is fine, so it must be fine.” How would you respond using what you have learned about conscience and objective truth?

This is one of the most frequent conversations in our time. Rehearse your response. It is not about winning an argument but about helping someone understand that conscience does not create truth, it discovers it.

Key Idea

Key Idea: Conscience is the inner sanctuary where God speaks. I must always obey it, but I must form it with God’s Word, the Church’s teaching, prayer, and examination. A well-formed conscience is the surest compass on the journey of life.

Cultural Wealth

Witness St. John Henry Newman (1801-1890) and his Grammar of Assent. Newman dedicated his life to exploring how conscience leads us to God. In his Grammar of Assent, he argues that conscience is not only a moral judge but the most powerful proof of God’s existence: the inner voice that commands us to do good presupposes a Legislator who gave it to us. His Letter to the Duke of Norfolk is the most brilliant defense of conscience in the Catholic tradition. Canonized in 2019 by Pope Francis.

Film & Literature St. Thomas More and “A Man for All Seasons” (Robert Bolt, 1960; film 1966 with Paul Scofield). When Henry VIII demanded everyone swear the Act of Supremacy recognizing him as head of the Church, Thomas More refused. He did not argue; he simply kept silent. His conscience, formed in the Catholic faith, prevented him from accepting what the king commanded. He was imprisoned in the Tower of London and beheaded. His last words were: “I die the king’s good servant, but God’s first.” One of the most powerful scenes in cinema is his dialogue with his daughter Margaret in prison, when she begs him to swear —even if he does not believe it— and he replies: “When a man takes an oath, he’s holding his own self in his own hands. Like water. And if he opens his fingers then —he needn’t hope to find himself again.”

Witness Dietrich Bonhoeffer (1906-1945) and his Ethics. German Lutheran pastor who, from his Christian conscience, opposed the Nazi regime. His book Ethics, written underground, explores the relationship between conscience, responsibility, and obedience to God. Bonhoeffer eventually participated in the conspiracy to overthrow Hitler —a heart-wrenching decision for a pacifist pastor— because his conscience told him that not acting was a worse sin. He was executed at Flossenbürg concentration camp in April 1945, just weeks before the war ended. His last words were: “This is the end. For me, the beginning of life.”

Film “Of Gods and Men” (Xavier Beauvois, 2010). The true story of eight Trappist monks in Algeria who, facing the threat of Islamic terrorists, had to decide whether to stay or flee. The film shows each monk’s process of discernment in conscience —their doubts, their fears, and finally their decision to stay. They died as martyrs in 1996. This is one of the most beautiful portrayals of how conscience functions in community that exists in cinema.

Popular Culture Pinocchio and Jiminy Cricket. “Let your conscience be your guide” sings Jiminy Cricket in the Disney film. Although it is a cartoon, the image captures something true: conscience is like a voice that is both external and internal —it comes from outside (from God) but resonates within. Pinocchio wants to be “a real boy,” and the path to achieving that is learning to listen to his conscience. Even in popular culture, conscience appears as the condition for being fully human.

Conciliar Document Gaudium et Spes 16. Reading this paragraph of the Second Vatican Council is reading one of the most sublime texts of the Catholic tradition on human dignity. “Conscience is the most secret core and sanctuary of a man. There he is alone with God, whose voice echoes in his depths.” This paragraph inspired CCC 1776 and deserves to be read slowly, meditated upon, and prayed over. It is available free at vatican.va.

Practice Ignatian Examen. In addition to the daily examen explained above, there are guides for examining conscience before Confession based on the Ten Commandments, the Beatitudes, or the capital sins. Apps like Hallow, Laudate, or Reimagining the Examen include guided examens. The YouCat (questions 295-300) also offers an examen adapted for young people.
